title: Explain HTTPS to a 12-year-old
response:
Imagine passing notes in class, except your note travels through twenty strangers' hands before reaching your friend. That's the internet — every message you send hops through machines owned by people you've never met.

HTTPS solves this with a trick that sounds impossible: you and the website agree on a secret code *while everyone is listening*, and nobody listening can figure it out. It works like a padlock the website mails you, already open. Anyone can snap a padlock shut on a box, but only the website holds the key that opens it. You lock your secret inside; eavesdroppers see only a sealed box.

Once you share that secret, everything gets scrambled — passwords, messages, embarrassing searches — into gibberish that would take longer than the age of the universe to crack by guessing.

One more trick: before any of this, the website shows ID — a certificate vouched for by organizations your browser already trusts — proving it's really your bank and not a fake dressed up to look like it. That's what the padlock icon in your address bar means: scrambled messages, delivered to someone who proved who they are.
scoreDetail: technical accuracy: 7/10, clarity for a 12-year-old: 7/10, analogy quality: 8/10, engagement: 8/10 — Vivid padlock/key-exchange analogy but slightly oversimplifies the key exchange mechanism (implies a shared padlock/key model closer to RSA than the actual Diffie-Hellman-style handshake HTTPS typically uses) and glosses over what makes a certificate authority trustworthy.
